elements.sent-2411-001: The flow of fluids within porous rocks is an important process with numerous applications in Earth sciences.
elements.sent-2411-002: Modeling the compaction-driven fluid flow requires the solution of coupled nonlinear partial differential equations that account for the fluid flow and the solid deformation within the porous medium.
elements.sent-2411-003: Despite the nonlinear relation of porosity and permeability that is commonly encountered, natural data show evidence of channelized fluid flow in rocks that have an overall layered structure.
elements.sent-2411-004: Layers of different rock types routinely have discontinuous hydraulic and mechanical properties.
elements.sent-2411-005: We present numerical results obtained by a novel space-time method which can handle discontinuous initial porosity (and permeability) distributions.
elements.sent-2411-006: The space-time method enables a straightforward coupling to models of mass transport for trace elements.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our results show the influence of different kinds of layering in the development of fluid-rich channels and mass transport.
elements.sent-2411-008: The flow of fluids in the Earth's subsurface is important for many applications.
elements.sent-2411-009: Examples of such applications include, but are not limited to, the migration of magma~\cite{McKenzie1984,Barcilon1986 , the flow of glaciers~\cite{Fowler1984 , the integrity of subsurface reservoirs~\cite{Raess2018,Yarushina2022 , and the efficiency of geothermal systems~\cite{Utkin2021 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: A distinctive aspect of the fluid flow within the deep Earth is that rocks cannot be treated as purely elastic or rigid, requiring consideration of their bulk (volumetric) viscous deformation~\cite{McKenzie1984,Scott1986 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In fact, recent experiments have confirmed that the viscous/viscoelastic behavior of rocks can be observed also at near-surface conditions~\cite{Sabitova2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Thus, the volumetric deformation and the associated fluid flow need to be considered in a coupled fashion since (de)compaction can drive fluid flow and vice versa~\cite{Connolly1998,Vasilyev1998 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: In the latter studies, porosity waves were observed numerically.
elements.sent-2411-014: Such waves reflect the propagation of porosity perturbations (and the associated volumetric deformation) in a wave-like fashion with minimal dissipation.
elements.sent-2411-015: The transport of fluid-filled porosity in a non-dissipative fashion has been at the focus of research by geoscientists since it has important implications for the geochemical anomalies that are observed near the surface of the Earth \cite{Richter1986,Navon1987,Jordan2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: The shape of porosity waves has been shown to depend very sensitively on the nonlinear behavior of the bulk (volumetric) viscosity.
elements.sent-2411-017: For example, in cases where the compaction/decompaction behavior is associated with significant changes in the effective viscosity, porosity waves take a channel-like shape (in two or three dimensions) that is responsible for the focusing of the flow towards the Earth's surface \cite{Raess2018,Connolly2007,Raess2014,Raess2019,Yarushina2015a,Yarushina2015b,Yarushina2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The focusing of the flow produces ``chimney-like'' features that resemble geophysical observations~\cite{Raess2018,Yarushina2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: The occurrence of such features is very important in the quantification of fluid flow and the associated geochemical anomalies~\cite{Spiegelman2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: An essential feature of geological formations is that rocks are typically found in layers (strata).
elements.sent-2411-021: The layers are often composed of rock types that have different physical properties, such as porosity and permeability.
elements.sent-2411-022: It is exactly this change in permeability that is responsible for the formation of geological reservoirs.
elements.sent-2411-023: For example, a typical underground reservoir must be composed of rocks of high porosity (and permeability) and must be covered by rocks of negligible porosity (and permeability) that act as a ``seal'' to the underlying rock units.
elements.sent-2411-024: This configuration typically requires the consideration of porosity (and permeability) jump discontinuities across the lithological boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, the methods used to solve the respective poro-viscoelastic equations numerically cannot handle a discontinuous initial porosity, and hence only approximate it by a continuous function with steep gradient.
elements.sent-2411-026: This approach leads to smoothing effects and does not preserve the discontinuous nature of solutions.
elements.sent-2411-027: Resolving the solution behavior next to a discontinuity is crucial in all the applications where the quantification of the fluid flow is needed and can thus be important for safety analyses in geoengineering applications~\cite{Yarushina2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Here, we consider a poro-viscoelastic model that generalizes the one introduced in~\cite{Connolly1998,Vasilyev1998 for the interaction of porosity and pressure.}
elements.sent-2411-029: For modeling sharp transitions between materials, as caused, for example, by stacked rock layers, it is important to be able to treat porosities with jump discontinuities.
elements.sent-2411-030: These discontinuities turn out to be determined mainly by the initial condition, as it was shown in~\cite{Bachmayr2023 based on results from~\cite{Simpson2006 for smooth initial porosities.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In addition, we utilize a newly-developed space-time method that has been shown to be more accurate in solving this particular problem in the presence of jump discontinuities~\cite{Bachmayr2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Our approach can be used to benchmark methods that do not include discontinuities and quantify the error between the two approaches.
elements.sent-2411-033: An additional advantage of the space-time method is that it can be coupled to simple models of chemical-tracer transport~\cite{Richter1986,Jordan2018 in a straightforward manner.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The results obtained from this coupling allow us to investigate the evolution of chemical anomalies in the presence of channelized fluid flow.
elements.sent-2411-035: In particular, our results are relevant to the formation of ore deposits and to the transport of trace elements in the subsurface.
elements.sent-2411-036: The model for poro-viscoelastic flow on which we focus in this work reads \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\partial_t \poro =-(1-\poro) \bl \poro^m \frac{\press}{\decWeak(\press)} +Q\partial_t \press \br ,\label{eq:model_a} \partial_t \press =\frac{1}{Q} \bl \nabla \cdot \frac{\perm}{\fluidVisc\backPoro^n} \poro^n (\nabla \press + (1-\poro) \densityDiff \gravity \Vec{e}_d)-\poro^m \frac{\press}{\bulkVisc\decWeak(\press)} \br,\label{eq:model_b}\end{equation} \end{subequations where $\poro$ is the porosity (void ratio), $\press$ is the effective pressure, $\decWeak$ accounts for decompaction weakening~\cite{Raess2018,Raess2019 , $Q$ is the compressibility (equal to $\bulkModulus^{-1}$, where $\bulkModulus$ is the bulk modulus), $\densityDiff = \solidDensity-\fluidDensity$ the density difference, and $\frac{\bulkVisc\decWeak {(1-\poro)\poro^m}$ can be regarded as effective viscosity similar to~\cite{Stevenson1991 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Finally, $t$ is time and $\Vec{e _d$ is the vector indicating the gravity acceleration direction (all symbols and the respective units are given in \cref{tab:variables ).}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The problem is furthermore supplemented with initial porosity $\poro_0\colon \Omega\to (0,1)$ and initial effective pressure $\press_0\colon \Omega\to \R$.

elements.sent-2411-058: To make this more precise we start by introducing the new variable $\poroTransform = - \log(1-\poro)$, so that $\poro = 1 - e^{-\poroTransform}$, because then the system~\eqref{eq:model can be written in the form \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\partial_t \poroTransform = - \bl \beta(\poroTransform) \frac{\press}{\decWeak(\press)} +Q\partial_t \press \br, \label{eq:transformedmodel_a} \partial_t \press = \frac{1}{Q} \bl \nabla \cdot \alpha(\poroTransform) (\nabla \press + \zeta(\poroTransform)) - \beta(\poroTransform) \frac{\press}{\decWeak(\press)} \br, \label{eq:transformedmodel_b}\end{equation} \end{subequations where \begin{equation}\alpha(\poroTransform) := \frac{\perm}{\fluidVisc\backPoro^n} (1-e^{-\poroTransform})^n,\quad \beta(\poroTransform) := \frac{1}{\bulkVisc} (1 - e^{-\poroTransform})^m,\quad \zeta(\poroTransform) := e^{-\poroTransform} \densityDiff \gravity \Vec{e}_d\,.\end{equation}.}}}

elements.sent-2411-121: We have presented results for the case of compaction-driven fluid flow in relation to fluid migration in the deep subsurface.

elements.sent-2411-148: Layers of different rock types have discontinuous hydraulic and mechanical properties.
elements.sent-2411-149: We present numerical results obtained by a novel space-time method, which can handle discontinuous initial porosity (and permeability) distributions \REV{efficiently .}
elements.sent-2411-150: The space-time method enables straightforward coupling to models of mass transport for trace elements.
elements.sent-2411-151: Finally, our results indicate that the enrichment of trace elements depends not only on the channelization of the flow but also on the interaction of fluid-filled channels with layers of different porosity and permeability .
elements.sent-2411-152: In addition, we utilize a newly-developed space-time method that has been shown to be more accurate in solving this particular problem in the presence of jump discontinuities~\cite{Bachmayr2025 .}
elements.sent-2411-153: An additional advantage of the space-time method is that it can be coupled to simple models of chemical-tracer transport \REV{(see, for example, ~\cite{Richter1986,Jordan2018}\REV{) as a post-processing step, since the entire porosity-pressure history is saved and the chemical transport problem does not feedback into the porosity-pressure (hydro-mechanical) model .}}
elements.sent-2411-154: The results obtained from this coupling allow us\REV{, for the first time, to investigate the evolution of chemical anomalies in the presence of channelized fluid flow\REV{, and their interactions with porosity/permeability discontinuities .}}
